page: Amazon Web Services d2.8xlarge is a dense storage instance powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2676 v3 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. It features 36 virtual CPUs (18 physical cores with Hyper-Threading) running at a clock speed of 2.4 GHz, backed by a 30 MB L3 cache. The instance is equipped with 244.0 GB of system memory, providing 6.78 GB per core. A key hardware highlight is its massive local storage capacity, consisting of 49152 GB of HDD storage, paired with a baseline network bandwidth of 10 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear divergence between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core CPU performance, measured via stress-ng and Geekbench, falls into the bottom 10%, reflecting the older architecture and modest clock speed. Single-threaded compression tasks also register as weak. However, multi-core execution, including multi-threaded bzip3 decompression and Geekbench multi-core tests, reaches average levels (middle 50%). Memory bandwidth and database operations, such as Redis SET and Passmark database tests, also perform within the average tier.

The primary tradeoff of the d2.8xlarge is its reliance on high-capacity HDD local storage rather than NVMe SSDs, which limits random I/O performance but offers exceptional resource density for sequential storage-heavy workloads. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the instance provides a massive volume of local storage per unit of compute and memory, making it highly efficient for sequential data access patterns. It is best suited for workloads such as large-scale data warehousing, MapReduce processing, Hadoop clusters, log processing, and massive sequential-access databases where storage volume outweighs the need for high single-core compute performance.
description: Amazon Web Services d2.8xlarge is a dense storage instance designed for high-capacity sequential storage workloads. Powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2676 v3 processor with 36 vCPUs (18 physical cores) running at 2.4 GHz, it includes 244.0 GB of memory and a massive 49152 GB of local HDD storage. Operating on the Xen hypervisor, the instance delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 10 Gbps. Benchmark data shows that while single-core performance is poor, multi-core capabilities, memory bandwidth, and database operations perform at average levels. The instance offers high qualitative cost efficiency for storage-dense configurations, trading off high-speed random I/O for massive sequential storage capacity. It is highly suited for MapReduce, Hadoop, log processing, and large-scale data warehousing.
og_description: Amazon Web Services d2.8xlarge is a dense storage instance featuring 36 vCPUs, 244.0 GB of RAM, and 49152 GB of local HDD storage, designed for sequential data workloads and MapReduce clusters.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services d2.8xlarge is a dense storage instance with 36 vCPUs, 244.0 GB of RAM, and 49152 GB of local HDD storage.
tagline: A dense storage instance delivering massive local HDD capacity and balanced multi-core performance for sequential data processing and large-scale analytical workloads.
bullet_points: 36 vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2676 v3 processor
Massive 49152 GB of local HDD storage for dense data requirements
244.0 GB of system memory and 10 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
Average multi-core and database performance despite weak single-core metrics
Optimized for MapReduce, Hadoop, and sequential log processing
